To discourage the Corinthians from fellowship with idolaters and security in any sinful behavior, the apostle presents to them the example of the Jewish nation of old. They were, by a miracle, led through the Red Sea, where the pursuing Egyptians were drowned. This was for them a typical baptism. The manna on which they fed was a type of Christ crucified, the Bread that came down from heaven, whoever eats it will live forever. Christ is the Rock on which the Christian church is built; and from the streams that flow from him, all believers drink and are refreshed. It typified the sacred influences of the Holy Spirit, as given to believers through Christ. But let none presume upon their great privileges or profession of the truth; these will not secure heavenly happiness.

Carnal desires grow stronger with indulgence, therefore they should be checked in their initial rise. Let us fear the sins of Israel if we want to avoid their plagues. And it is just to fear that those who tempt Christ will be left by him in the power of the old serpent. Murmuring against God’s arrangements and commands greatly provokes him. Nothing in Scripture is written without purpose; and it is our wisdom and duty to learn from it. Others have fallen, and so may we. A Christian's security against sin is distrust of himself. God has not promised to keep us from falling if we do not look to ourselves. To this word of caution, a word of comfort is added. Others experience similar burdens and temptations: what they endure and overcome, we also may. God is wise as well as faithful, and will make our burdens according to our strength. He knows what we can bear. He will make a way to escape; he will deliver either from the trial itself or at least its consequences. We have full encouragement to flee from sin and to be faithful to God. We cannot fall by temptation if we hold fast to him. Whether the world smiles or frowns, it is an enemy; but believers will be strengthened to overcome it, with all its terrors and enticements. The fear of the Lord, put into their hearts, will be a great means of safety.

Did not participating in the Lord’s supper demonstrate a profession of faith in Christ crucified and adoring gratitude to him for his salvation? Christians, by this ordinance and the faith therein professed, were united as grains of wheat in one loaf of bread or as members in the human body, since they were all united to Christ and had fellowship with him and one another. This is confirmed from Jewish worship and customs regarding sacrifice. The apostle applies this to feasting with idolaters. Eating food as part of a heathen sacrifice was worshipping the idol to whom it was made and having fellowship or communion with it; just as he who eats the Lord’s supper is accounted to partake in the Christian sacrifice, or as those who ate the Jewish sacrifices partook of what was offered on their altar. It was denying Christianity; for fellowship with Christ and fellowship with devils cannot be had at once. If Christians venture into places and join in sacrifices to the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eye, and the pride of life, they will provoke God.

There were situations where Christians might eat what had been offered to idols without sin. Such as when the meat was sold in the market as common food, for the priest to whom it had been given. But a Christian must not merely consider what is lawful, but what is expedient and to edify others. Christianity by no means forbids the common acts of kindness or allows discourteous behavior to anyone, however they may differ from us in religious sentiments or practices. But this is not to be understood of religious festivals, participating in idolatrous worship. According to this advice of the apostle, Christians should take care not to use their liberty to the detriment of others or to their own shame. In eating and drinking, and in all we do, we should aim at the glory of God, at pleasing and honoring him. This is the great purpose of all religion, and directs us where explicit rules are lacking. A holy, peaceful, and benevolent spirit will disarm even the greatest enemies.
